name: api-patterns
description: API design principles and decision-making. REST vs GraphQL vs tRPC selection, response formats, versioning, pagination.
capabilities: Guide selection between REST, GraphQL, and tRPC based on project requirements
Provide HTTP status code reference for proper error handling
Explain API versioning strategies (URI, header, query parameters)
Detail response formatting patterns including pagination and envelope wrappers
Outline security testing approaches for API endpoints
Suggest authentication patterns (JWT, OAuth, API keys, passkeys)
author: sickn33
securitySummary: All 67 static findings are false positives. The skill is a documentation resource for API design patterns. Backticks detected are markdown code formatting, not shell commands. No weak cryptography or reconnaissance tools present.
valueStatement: Building APIs without clear design principles leads to inconsistent interfaces and poor developer experience. This skill provides decision trees and best practices for choosing the right API style and implementing it correctly.
userTitle: Design Better APIs with Pattern Guidance
limitations: Does not write actual API code - provides guidance only
Does not include implementation examples in specific frameworks
Does not cover database schema design
Does not provide deployment or infrastructure guidance

useCases.targetUser: Backend developers and architects evaluating API options
useCases.title: Choosing API Architecture
useCases.description: Developer starting a new project and needs to decide between REST, GraphQL, or tRPC based on their frontend stack and data complexity.

useCases.title: Designing REST Endpoints
useCases.description: Team implementing a REST API and needs guidance on resource naming, HTTP methods, and proper status code usage.

useCases.title: API Security Review
useCases.description: Security engineer auditing existing APIs for common vulnerabilities using OWASP API Top 10 checklist.

promptTemplates.title: API Style Selection
promptTemplates.prompt: I am building a [describe your project, e.g., e-commerce app with React frontend and Node.js backend]. What API style should I use? What are the trade-offs?
promptTemplates.scenario: Choosing between REST, GraphQL, or tRPC for a new project

promptTemplates.title: REST Endpoint Design
promptTemplates.prompt: I need to design REST endpoints for [describe resource, e.g., user management with profiles, posts, and comments]. Show me the proper URL structure, HTTP methods, and status codes.
promptTemplates.scenario: Designing REST API endpoints for a specific domain

promptTemplates.title: API Error Handling
promptTemplates.prompt: What is the best way to handle errors in my API? Show me the proper response format and which HTTP status codes to use for [list error scenarios].

promptTemplates.title: API Versioning Strategy
promptTemplates.prompt: How should I version my API? I expect the API to evolve over time. Compare URI versioning, header versioning, and query parameter versioning.

bestPractices: Choose API style based on client needs, not personal preference - consider frontend stack, data complexity, and caching requirements
Use consistent response envelopes and standard error formats across all endpoints
Implement rate limiting from the start and communicate limits clearly in API documentation
antiPatterns: Using verbs in REST endpoints (e.g., /getUsers instead of GET /users)
Returning different response formats for the same resource across different endpoints
Exposing internal error messages and stack traces to API clients

faq.question: When should I choose REST over GraphQL?
faq.answer: Choose REST for public APIs needing wide compatibility, simple CRUD operations, or when HTTP caching is important. Choose GraphQL for complex data relationships, multiple frontend platforms, or when clients need flexible queries.

faq.question: What is the best API versioning strategy?
faq.answer: URI versioning (e.g., /v1/users) is most common and visible. Header versioning is cleaner but less discoverable. Query versioning (e.g., /users?version=1) works for minor changes but can clutter URLs.

faq.question: How should I handle pagination in my API?
faq.answer: Use cursor-based pagination for large datasets and infinite scroll. Use offset-based pagination for known page sizes. Include total count when useful and provide next/previous links in the response.

faq.question: What is the difference between 401 and 403 status codes?
faq.answer: 401 Unauthorized means authentication is missing or invalid. 403 Forbidden means authentication is valid but the user lacks permission for the requested resource.

faq.question: Should I use JWT or session-based authentication?
faq.answer: JWT is better for stateless APIs and microservices. Session-based auth works well for traditional server-rendered apps. Consider your scaling needs and client types.

faq.question: How do I protect my API from common attacks?
faq.answer: Implement rate limiting, validate all input, use parameterized queries, implement proper authorization checks (BOLA), disable introspection in production, and follow OWASP API Top 10 guidelines.
